covered_sections: 1.3. Types of asexual reproduction>1.3.4. Vegetative reproduction
1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention>null

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which birth control method is 100% effective at preventing pregnancy and also prevents transmission of sexually transmitted infections?
questions.hint: This method avoids any contact between sperm and egg.
questions.explanation: Abstinence from sexual intercourse completely prevents sperm from meeting an egg, providing 100% pregnancy prevention and STI protection. Condoms and IUDs do not offer 100% protection, and pills do not prevent STIs.

source_trace.content_anchor: This is the most obvious way of preventing a pregnancy . . . effective method with added advantage of preventing exposure to sexually transmissible pathogens

explanations_tiered.definition: Abstinence is the deliberate avoidance of sexual intercourse, providing complete protection against pregnancy and STIs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Abstinence is 100% effective because no sperm can reach an egg; it also eliminates any risk of STI transmission through sexual contact.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Not having sex at all means there's no chance of pregnancy or catching an STI — it's the only guaranteed method.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Abstinence from sexual intercourse prevents any possibility of sperm-egg contact, achieving 100% effectiveness for pregnancy prevention and STI prevention simultaneously.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Abstinence provides absolute contraceptive efficacy (Pearl Index 0) because the necessary condition for fertilization (sperm in female reproductive tract) is never met, and STI transmission requires mucosal contact absent with abstinence.
reasoning_steps: Recall that pregnancy requires sperm to meet egg.
Abstinence prevents any contact between sperm and egg.
Condoms and pills can fail; IUD does not prevent STIs.
misconceptions: Abstinence is sometimes confused with withdrawal (coitus interruptus), which is not effective.
Some believe abstinence is only for religious reasons — it is also the most effective medical method.

glossary_entry.definition: Deliberate avoidance of sexual intercourse, providing complete protection against pregnancy and STIs.

flashcard.front: Which birth control method is 100% effective against pregnancy and STIs?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the only 100% effective method for preventing both pregnancy and STIs?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Complete avoidance of sexual intercourse, providing total protection.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that other methods reduce risk but do not eliminate it completely.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy and STIs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Only abstinence guarantees 100% effectiveness against both pregnancy and STIs; condoms reduce risk but can fail, pills do not prevent STIs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the primary mechanism by which a male condom prevents pregnancy?

options.A: It traps sperm and prevents them from entering the uterus
options.B: It releases hormones that prevent ovulation
options.C: It kills sperm by releasing a chemical spermicide
options.D: It prevents implantation of the embryo in the uterus

questions.hint: Condoms act as a physical barrier.
questions.explanation: A condom is a thin rubber sheath placed on the erect penis that traps sperm, preventing them from reaching the uterus and fallopian tubes. Option B describes hormonal pills, option C describes spermicides, and option D describes IUDs.

source_trace.content_anchor: traps the sperm and prevents them from reaching the uterus

explanations_tiered.definition: A condom is a barrier contraceptive that physically traps sperm, preventing entry into the female reproductive tract.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The condom collects semen, keeping sperm out of the vagina, cervix, and uterus, thus preventing fertilization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A condom is like a glove that catches the sperm so they can't swim up to meet the egg.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The male condom acts as a physical barrier, trapping sperm inside the sheath and preventing them from entering the cervix and reaching the oviduct for fertilization.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Condoms function by mechanical collection of ejaculate, preventing sperm from accessing cervical mucus; their effectiveness depends on correct use and absence of breakage or leakage.
reasoning_steps: Condoms are barrier methods.
They physically block sperm from entering the uterus.
Eliminate options about hormones (pills), spermicides (chemicals), or implantation (IUD).
misconceptions: Condoms do not release hormones or chemicals unless combined with spermicide.
Condoms prevent fertilization, not implantation.

glossary_entry.definition: A thin rubber sheath placed on the penis that traps sperm, preventing pregnancy.

flashcard.front: How does a male condom prevent pregnancy?
flashcard.back: It traps sperm, preventing them from entering the uterus.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the mechanism of a male condom?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It traps sperm, preventing them from entering the uterus and reaching the egg.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Also note that condoms reduce STI transmission, unlike hormonal methods.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Condoms trap sperm; hormonal methods prevent ovulation; spermicides kill sperm; IUDs prevent implantation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why do combined oral contraceptive pills prevent pregnancy effectively when taken daily?

options.A: They thicken cervical mucus and thin the uterine lining
options.B: They kill sperm before they can reach the egg
options.C: They suppress ovulation by maintaining high estrogen and progesterone levels
options.D: They prevent the zygote from implanting in the uterus

questions.hint: The pill mimics pregnancy hormone levels to stop egg release.
questions.explanation: Combined oral contraceptives contain synthetic estrogen and progesterone, which suppress FSH and LH release from the pituitary, preventing ovulation. Option A describes some effects but the primary mechanism is ovulation suppression.

source_trace.content_anchor: suppress ovulation and so prevent conception

explanations_tiered.definition: Combined oral contraceptives contain synthetic estrogen and progesterone that prevent ovulation by inhibiting pituitary FSH and LH release.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The pill maintains high hormone levels, mimicking pregnancy, which signals the pituitary to stop releasing FSH and LH, so follicles do not mature and no egg is released.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pill tricks the body into thinking it's already pregnant, so no egg is released from the ovary.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Combined oral contraceptives suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis: estrogen inhibits FSH (preventing follicle development), and progesterone inhibits LH (preventing ovulation).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The negative feedback of exogenous estrogen and progesterone on the hypothalamus reduces GnRH pulsatility, lowering FSH and LH to levels insufficient for follicular development and the ovulatory LH surge.
reasoning_steps: Recall that ovulation requires an LH surge triggered by rising estrogen.
The pill provides steady estrogen and progesterone, preventing the mid-cycle LH surge.
Without LH surge, no egg is released.
misconceptions: The pill does not kill sperm; it prevents ovulation.
Some think the pill works after fertilization — it prevents ovulation, not implantation.

glossary_entry.definition: Hormonal contraceptive that prevents ovulation by suppressing FSH and LH.

flashcard.front: How does the birth control pill prevent pregnancy?
flashcard.back: It suppresses ovulation by maintaining high estrogen and progesterone levels, inhibiting FSH and LH.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the main mechanism of the combined pill?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Suppresses ovulation by inhibiting FSH and LH release.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Hormonal birth control that prevents ovulation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The pill suppresses ovulation via hormone feedback; other effects are secondary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is a vasectomy?

options.A: Cutting and sealing the vas deferens to prevent sperm from entering the semen
options.B: Removal of the testes to stop sperm and testosterone production
options.C: Cutting the urethra to block the passage of semen
options.D: Tying the oviducts to prevent eggs from reaching the uterus

questions.hint: Vasectomy involves the tubes that carry sperm.
questions.explanation: Vasectomy is a surgical procedure where the vas deferens (sperm ducts) are cut and sealed, preventing sperm from being included in the ejaculate. Option B is castration, C is not performed, and D is female sterilization (tubal ligation).

source_trace.content_anchor: man's sperm ducts are cut and the ends sealed

explanations_tiered.definition: Vasectomy is a permanent male sterilization procedure that cuts and seals the vas deferens, preventing sperm from entering the semen.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The vas deferens is severed and sealed, so sperm cannot reach the urethra; testosterone production and ejaculation are unaffected.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A vasectomy cuts the tubes that carry sperm, so the liquid that comes out during sex has no sperm in it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vasectomy involves bilateral vas deferens ligation and transection, which blocks sperm transport from the epididymis to the urethra while preserving testicular endocrine function.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The procedure occludes the ductus deferens, typically via excision and fascial interposition; sperm are produced but absorbed in the epididymis, and semen volume remains unchanged (5% sperm, 95% glandular secretions).
reasoning_steps: Recall that vasectomy is a male procedure.
Identify the vas deferens as the sperm transport duct.
Cutting/sealing it blocks sperm from semen.
misconceptions: Vasectomy does not remove the testes or affect testosterone — masculinity and sex drive remain.
Vasectomy does not prevent ejaculation; semen is still produced but without sperm.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vasectomy: cut vas deferens → no sperm in semen.

glossary_entry.definition: Surgical male sterilization by cutting and sealing the vas deferens.

flashcard.back: It cuts and seals the vas deferens, preventing sperm from entering the semen.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Vasectomy cuts the vas deferens; testes remain; urethra unaffected; tubal ligation is female.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Surgical cutting/sealing of the vas deferens to prevent sperm from entering semen.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Male sterilization procedure blocking sperm transport.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that it does not affect hormones or sexual function.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss methods of prevention of pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Vasectomy cuts the vas deferens; tubal ligation cuts oviducts (female).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: What does HIV target and destroy in the human body?
questions.hint: HIV attacks the body's defense system.
questions.explanation: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) specifically infects and destroys CD4+ T cells (helper T cells), which are crucial for immune function. This leads to immunosuppression and eventually AIDS. Option A is incorrect; HIV does not target red blood cells or nerve cells primarily.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: HIV kills immune cells in the body

explanations_tiered.definition: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) is a retrovirus that infects and destroys CD4+ T lymphocytes, weakening the immune system.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: HIV binds to CD4 receptors on helper T cells, replicates inside them, and causes cell death, leading to progressive immune deficiency.
explanations_tiered.beginner: HIV attacks the body's soldier cells (immune cells), making it hard to fight off other infections.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HIV targets CD4+ T cells, which coordinate immune responses. Destruction of these cells leads to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS).
explanations_tiered.advanced: HIV-1 integrates into the host genome of CD4+ T cells, using reverse transcriptase. Chronic depletion of these cells results in opportunistic infections and AIDS.
reasoning_steps: Recall that HIV stands for Human Immunodeficiency Virus.
'Immunodeficiency' means it damages the immune system.
Immune cells (CD4+ T cells) are the target.
misconceptions: HIV does not cause AIDS immediately; it may take years.
HIV is not transmitted by casual contact; it requires blood, semen, vaginal fluid, or breast milk exposure.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: HIV destroys immune cells (CD4+ T cells).

glossary_entry.definition: Human Immunodeficiency Virus, which attacks CD4+ T cells and causes AIDS.

flashcard.front: What type of cells does HIV destroy?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Virus that destroys immune cells, leading to AIDS.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that HIV is not the same as AIDS; AIDS is the late stage.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe types and transmission of STIs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HIV targets CD4+ T cells; red blood cells, nerve cells, and sperm cells are not primary targets.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: Which bacterium causes syphilis?
questions.hint: Syphilis is caused by a spiral-shaped bacterium.
questions.explanation: Syphilis is caused by the spiral-shaped bacterium Treponema pallidum. Option A causes gonorrhea, option C causes chlamydia, and option D is a protozoan causing trichomoniasis.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: caused by Treponema pallidum, a spiral shaped bacterium

explanations_tiered.definition: Syphilis is a bacterial STI caused by the spirochete Treponema pallidum.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Treponema pallidum enters through mucous membranes or skin breaks, causing chancres, then systemic spread if untreated.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A specific kind of corkscrew-shaped bacteria called Treponema pallidum causes syphilis.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Treponema pallidum is the etiologic agent of syphilis, a spirochete bacterium transmitted sexually through direct contact with a chancre.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Treponema pallidum subspecies pallidum is a motile spirochete that cannot be cultured in vitro; it invades via intact mucous membranes and disseminates hematogenously.
reasoning_steps: Recall the specific bacterial name for syphilis.
Treponema pallidum is the only spiral bacterium listed.
Eliminate Neisseria (gonorrhea), Chlamydia, and Trichomonas (protozoan).
misconceptions: Syphilis is not caused by the same bacterium as gonorrhea or chlamydia.
Syphilis can be cured with antibiotics (penicillin) if caught early.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Differentiate bacterial STIs (syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia) from viral (HIV, herpes) and protozoan (trichomoniasis).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Treponema pallidum causes syphilis; Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes gonorrhea; Chlamydia trachomatis causes chlamydia; Trichomonas vaginalis causes trichomoniasis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: Which behavior most effectively reduces the risk of HIV transmission during sexual activity?

options.C: Using a condom correctly for every sexual act

questions.hint: Barrier protection is key for STI prevention.
questions.explanation: Consistent and correct condom use provides a barrier against HIV and other STIs, reducing transmission risk by approximately 80-95%. Option A increases breakage risk; B does not prevent STIs; D (withdrawal) offers no protection against HIV.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: risk can be greatly reduced if the man uses a condom

explanations_tiered.definition: Condom use is an effective barrier method for reducing HIV transmission during sexual intercourse.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Correct and consistent condom use prevents exposure to HIV-containing body fluids (semen, vaginal fluid), significantly reducing transmission risk.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Using a condom every time you have sex creates a wall that stops HIV from passing from one person to another.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Consistent condom use provides a physical barrier that prevents exchange of HIV-infected fluids, reducing transmission risk by over 80% compared to unprotected sex.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Epidemiological studies show consistent condom use reduces HIV incidence by approximately 85-95%; the latex barrier prevents viral particles from reaching mucosal surfaces where transmission occurs.
reasoning_steps: Identify that HIV is transmitted through body fluids.
Condoms create a physical barrier, preventing fluid exchange.
Eliminate ineffective methods (withdrawal, pills) and risky practices (double condoms).
misconceptions: Two condoms are not better than one — friction increases breakage risk.
Oral contraceptives prevent pregnancy, not HIV.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: HIV prevention: correct condom use every time.

glossary_entry.definition: Strategies to avoid HIV transmission, including condom use, abstinence, and sterile needles.

flashcard.front: How can HIV transmission be reduced during sex?
flashcard.back: Using a condom correctly for every sexual act.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the most effective sexual behavior to reduce HIV risk?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Consistent and correct condom use for every sexual act.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Use of condoms or abstinence to prevent HIV transmission.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that abstinence is 100% effective but condoms are highly effective when used correctly.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Correct condom use is effective; double condoms increase breakage; pills don't prevent STIs; withdrawal offers no protection.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: How do gonorrhea and chlamydia differ in their long-term effects if left untreated?

options.A: Only gonorrhea causes pelvic inflammatory disease
options.B: Both can cause infertility in males and females

questions.hint: Both bacterial STIs can scar the reproductive tract.
questions.explanation: Both gonorrhea (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) and chlamydia (caused by Chlamydia trachomatis) can ascend the reproductive tract, causing pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) in females and epididymitis in males, leading to scarring and infertility in both sexes. Option B is correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: can scar the reproductive tract and lead to infertility in both sexes

explanations_tiered.definition: Gonorrhea and chlamydia are bacterial STIs that can both cause reproductive tract scarring and infertility if untreated.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Both infections can ascend from the lower to upper reproductive tract, causing PID in women and epididymitis in men, leading to blocked tubes and infertility.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Both gonorrhea and chlamydia can damage the tubes that carry eggs or sperm, making it hard or impossible to have children later.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Untreated gonorrhea and chlamydia both cause inflammation and scarring: in females, fallopian tube obstruction (PID); in males, vas deferens or epididymal blockage.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chronic infection with either pathogen induces a fibrotic inflammatory response; in females, this leads to tubal factor infertility; in males, obstructive azoospermia from epididymal fibrosis.
reasoning_steps: Recall that both are bacterial STIs that can ascend the tract.
Both cause inflammation and scarring.
Scarring of reproductive tubes leads to infertility in both sexes.
misconceptions: Some believe only females are affected by infertility from these STIs — males can also become infertile from epididymal scarring.
Gonorrhea and chlamydia can be asymptomatic, so untreated infections often go unnoticed.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Both gonorrhea & chlamydia → infertility if untreated.

glossary_entry.definition: Bacterial STI that can cause infertility in both sexes if untreated.

flashcard.front: What long-term effect can both gonorrhea and chlamydia cause?
flashcard.back: Infertility in both males and females due to reproductive tract scarring.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Both can cause infertility; neither is exclusive in causing PID; both cause long-term damage.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What common long-term effect do gonorrhea and chlamydia share?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Both can cause infertility in males and females if untreated.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Bacterial STI that can cause infertility.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that early treatment with antibiotics prevents permanent damage.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe causes and effects of common STIs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Both can cause infertility; option A is false (both can cause PID), C is false (both cause infertility), D is false (both cause damage).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: A person believes that HIV can be transmitted by sharing a drinking glass with an infected person. Which correction should be made?

options.A: HIV is transmitted only through unprotected sex
options.B: HIV is transmitted through blood, semen, vaginal fluid, and breast milk, but not through casual contact or saliva
options.C: HIV can be transmitted through any body fluid including sweat
options.D: HIV is only transmitted from mother to child during birth

questions.hint: HIV requires direct access to the bloodstream or mucous membranes.
questions.explanation: HIV is transmitted through specific infected body fluids: blood, semen, vaginal secretions, and breast milk. It is not transmitted through casual contact, saliva, sweat, tears, or sharing utensils. Option B correctly identifies transmission routes while correcting the misconception.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: spread from an infected person through blood or body fluids. This can occur through direct contact with infected blood or body fluids

explanations_tiered.definition: HIV is transmitted through infected blood, semen, vaginal fluid, and breast milk; not through casual contact, saliva, or shared utensils.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The virus must enter the bloodstream or mucous membranes; it is not present in sufficient quantity or viability in saliva, sweat, or on surfaces to cause infection.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You cannot get HIV from sharing a cup, hugging, or sitting on a toilet seat — the virus needs to get into your blood directly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: HIV transmission requires exchange of specific body fluids (blood, semen, vaginal secretions, breast milk) via mucosal membranes or bloodstream; casual contact poses no risk.
explanations_tiered.advanced: HIV is fragile outside the body; it is inactivated by drying, heat, and oxidation. Transmission requires a sufficient viral load in a fluid that contacts CD4+ cells, which does not occur with saliva or shared objects.
reasoning_steps: Identify the misconception: casual contact transmits HIV.
Recall the actual transmission fluids: blood, semen, vaginal fluid, breast milk.
Correct statement must exclude casual contact and specify true routes.
misconceptions: HIV is not transmitted by mosquitoes, shared toilets, or kissing.
Saliva contains negligible HIV and contains antiviral enzymes (lysozyme).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: HIV: blood, semen, vaginal fluid, breast milk only.

glossary_entry.definition: Spread of HIV through infected blood, semen, vaginal fluid, or breast milk.

flashcard.front: Can HIV be transmitted by sharing a drinking glass?
flashcard.back: No — HIV is only transmitted through blood, semen, vaginal fluid, and breast milk, not saliva or casual contact.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is HIV transmitted by sharing a drinking glass?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No — only through blood, semen, vaginal fluid, and breast milk.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Spread only via specific infected body fluids.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: HIV: only blood, semen, vaginal fluid, breast milk.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to address common public misconceptions and reduce stigma.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe modes of transmission of STIs
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HIV requires blood, semen, vaginal fluid, or breast milk; casual contact and saliva do not transmit the virus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.section: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
questions.question: Trichomoniasis is caused by which type of pathogen?
questions.hint: This STI is caused by a single-celled eukaryotic parasite.
questions.explanation: Trichomoniasis is caused by the flagellated protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis, not a bacterium, virus, or fungus. Option D correctly identifies the protozoan nature of this pathogen.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.11. Sexually transmitted infection: transmission and prevention
source_trace.content_anchor: caused by the flagellated protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis

explanations_tiered.definition: Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the protozoan parasite Trichomonas vaginalis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This flagellated protozoan infects the urogenital tract, causing discharge and itching; it is treated with antiprotozoal drugs like metronidazole.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Trichomoniasis is caused by a tiny one-celled parasite, not a bacterium or virus — a protozoan.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Trichomonas vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan (eukaryotic parasite), making trichomoniasis a protozoan STI distinct from bacterial or viral infections.
explanations_tiered.advanced: T. vaginalis is an anaerobic, flagellated parasitic protist that adheres to urogenital epithelial cells via adhesins and causes cytopathic effects; it lacks mitochondria but possesses hydrogenosomes.
reasoning_steps: Recall the specific organism: Trichomonas vaginalis.
Recognize '-omonas' indicates protozoan (related to Giardia, etc.).
Eliminate bacterium (gonorrhea), virus (HIV), fungus (candida).
misconceptions: Trichomoniasis is not bacterial; antibiotics do not work; antiprotozoals are needed.
It is not caused by poor hygiene but by sexual transmission.

glossary_entry.definition: STI caused by the protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis.

flashcard.front: What type of pathogen causes trichomoniasis?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Trichomonas vaginalis is a protozoan; bacteria cause syphilis/gonorrhea/chlamydia; viruses cause HIV/herpes; fungi cause candidiasis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is trichomoniasis bacterial, viral, fungal, or protozoan?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: STI caused by a protozoan parasite.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with bacterial STIs (syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia) and viral (HIV, herpes).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Trichomonas vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan, not a bacterium, virus, or fungus.
